content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 29, 2011
Tom Butt
City Council Member
117 Park Place
Richmond, CA 94801

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-11-115

Dear Mr. Butt:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. Also, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice.² (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 72.)

QUESTION

Does the Act permit you to participate in a decision regarding the re-zoning of the north shore area in Richmond?

CONCLUSION

No. It appears from your facts that you have an economic interest in a business entity that is the subject of the decision.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² We base the advice below on the information you provided in your request for advice – we have taken into account the information provided by SSL Law Firm, representing Richmond Development Corp. Given the complexity of the business relationships, we have made every attempt to analyze the facts based on the several letters we received.

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 2

FACTS

Interactive Resources ("IRI"): You are the Vice Mayor for the City of Richmond.
You are also the president of IRI, an architectural firm organized as a California corporation.
You have a 32.58 percent interest in that firm. You assume for purposes of this advice that your
interest in IRI has a value of more than $2,000.

Baltic Development Associates ("BDA"): You are a limited partner in BDA with a
10.23 percent interest. You assume for purposes of this advice that your interest in BDA has a
value of more than $2,000. BDA was created as a limited partnership in 1985. It currently has
several limited partners, including yourself, that collectively hold a 100 percent interest in the
partnership. IRI is the general partner of BDA for purposes of organization. You have stated
that IRI has no financial interest or decision-making authority in BDA.

Mariner Square Joint Venture ("MSJV") and Baltic Square Joint Venture
("BSJV"): BDA entered into two joint ventures with Richmond Development Company
("RDC"): MSJV and BSJV. BDA and RDC each have a 50 percent interest in each joint
venture.

Richmond Development Company ("RDC") and Genser & Watkins, LLP: Josh
Genser has a controlling interest in and manages RDC and is also a partner in the law firm
Genser & Watkins, LLP. The law firm rents office space from BSJV. This office space is also
the location for RDC. Mr. Genser manages RDC from his law firm offices, which is RDC's
principal place of business. RDC and the law firm do not have commingled funds or assets.
You have no ownership interest in RDC and Genser & Watkins, LLP. The firm pays about
$2,600 per month in rent to BSJV and that your pro rata share of BDA's 50 percent share would
be $1,595 per year.

The Richmond City Council will be considering and voting on a revised General Plan for
Richmond. Included in this plan will be changes in zoning to the north shoreline of Richmond.
Among the city council decisions will be whether or not to change the current designation of
portions of the north shoreline from Industrial/Office Flex to Open Space. The values of the
properties with designated zoning will likely be affected. RDC owns property on the north
shoreline and opposes re-designation. You have stated that the city council will decide "the
Northshore matter" on July 5, 2011. We understand from your facts that this decision will be
made separately from other issues related to the general plan and that there is no alleged conflict
in other decisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 3

Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in any given governmental decision.

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section 82048.) As a member of the
City Council, you are a public official within the meaning of the Act.³

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision-maker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) You would be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision when taking part in a City Council decision regarding Richmond's north
shore.

Step Three: What are your economic interests?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703.1(b));

³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200, which specifically includes a city councilmember,
and the official has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, the official must: (1) verbally
identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as
discussed in Regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting and immediately prior to the discussion of
the item; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5 subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

You have explained several business relationships that may be an economic interest to
you. We discuss each in turn.

Interactive Resources, Inc.: you have a 32.58 percent interest in this architectural firm.
You are also its president. You have an economic interest in IRI as a business entity and as a
source of income.

Baltic Development Associates: you have a 10.23 percent interest in this limited liability
partnership. You have stated that this interest is worth more than $2,000. There are several
limited partners involved and none has a controlling interest. You have an economic interest in
BDA as a business entity.

Baltic Square Joint Venture: You have an economic interest in BDA, which is a 50
percent owner of BSJV. RDC is the other 50 percent owner. You have stated that your 10.23
percent interest in BDA would give you an investment worth more than $2,000 in BSJV. You
have an economic interest in the BSJV as a business entity.

Any lessee⁴ of the properties that BSJV owns would be an economic interest as a source
of income to BDA. The amount of income would be calculated to account for BDA's 50 percent
ownership of the BSJV and your 10.23 percent interest in BDA. For example, if a tenant pays
$1,000 in rent, $500 of that is attributed to BDA and 10.23 percent of that $500 would be
attributed as income, or $51.15.⁵

⁴ See below for discussion of Genser & Watkins, LLP, as a lessee of BSJV.

⁵ Please note that this is the calculation whether the joint ventures turn a net profit or not. We look only at
the gross income when analyzing potential economic interests. We also realize that you have stated you have a
5.115 percent interest in each joint venture. We do not see a reason to split your interest in half for this purpose.
Rather, you would analyze a 10.23 percent interest in the income each receives.

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 5

Mariner Square Joint Venture: MSJV is the same as BSJV above, except for the
additional fact that MSJV paid IRI $332.66 for services rendered within the last 12 months.
Your interest in Interactive Resources is 32.58 percent and that amount would be adjusted
accordingly to $108.38. To be an economic interest as a source of income, the amount must total
at least $500 in the past 12 months. This amount does not meet the threshold under the Act.
You have not referred to any other payments specific to MSJV.

Genser & Watkins, LLP: Genser & Watkins LLP is a legal partnership composed of two
joint (50 percent) partners, including Joshua Genser. The law firm leases office space from
BSJV. Genser & Watkins, LLP is a source of income to BDA using the same calculation as
above: 50 percent of that income is attributed to BDA, and of that, 10.23 percent is attributed to
you. The firm pays about $2,600 per month in rent. Your pro rata share of BDA's 50% share
would be about $1,595 per year.

RDC: You do not have an economic interest in RDC itself, even though there is a
business relationship between one of your economic interests and this company. There is a
relationship, however, between RDC and Genser & Watkins, LLP.

Mr. Genser has a 20 percent interest in the RDC. He runs that business out of his law
office, using his firm's legal staff and resources. He is also the manager and day-to-day
decision-maker for RDC.

Regulation 18703.1(d)(2) provides:

"Business entities, including corporations, partnerships, joint ventures and any
other organizations and enterprises operated for profit, which do not have a
parent-subsidiary relationship are otherwise related if any one of the following
three tests is met:

"(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in the other
business entity.

"(B) There is shared management and control between the entities. In
determining whether there is shared management and control, consideration
should be given to the following factors:

"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages the
two entities;

"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets;

"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees, or
otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on a regular basis;

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 6

"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between the
entities; or

"(C) A controlling owner (50 percent or greater interest as a shareholder or as a
general partner) in one entity also is a controlling owner in the other entity."

RDC is managed by Josh Genser who is a partner in Genser & Watkins, LLP.
The businesses use the same principal place of business, the same staff, and Mr. Genser is
a manager of RDC and partner in Genser & Watkins. These two entities are "otherwise
related." You therefore have an interest in RDC as it is related to Genser & Watkins, a
source of income to BDA.

To summarize, you have an economic interest in IRI, BDA, Genser & Watkins,
LLP, BSJV, and MSJV. You do not have a direct economic interest in RDC. RDC is
otherwise related to Genser & Watkins, LLP, however, and RDC therefore "stands in the
shoes" of the firm and any interest in the firm is imputed to RDC.

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

A business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency
when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation
18704.1(a).)

None of the listed economic interests above have initiated the proceeding or are
named parties in the decision. None are "the subject" of the proceeding, except RDC to
the extent that the decision involves "other entitlement to" the property. Because the
decision involves a possible re-zoning of one of RDC's holdings, it could be "the subject
of the decision."

While "other entitlement" is not specifically defined, the California Court of
Appeal in City of Agoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation Com., (1988) 198 Cal. App.
3d 480, 497-498 explained in the context of campaign contribution/disqualification that
"entitlement for use" does not cover proceedings in which general policy decisions or
rules are made or where the interests affected are many and diverse. (Citing Fallon

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 7

Advice Letter, No. A-85-050.) Analogously, the "other entitlement" language above
would apply to situations where "the interests are many and diverse."6

Richmond's north shore area consists of approximately 100 acres and is split into
properties held by 13 entities. Four of these property owners have expressed that they
oppose the re-zoning of Richmond's north shore. Given these small numbers and lack
of evidence of "diversity of interests," we find that your economic interest in RDC as a
source of income is directly involved in the decision on the north shore.7

The remaining economic interests, above, are indirectly involved in the decision.

Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met?

For a business entity that is directly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation
18705.1 (b)(1) states unless the exception in subdivision (b)(2) applies, "the financial effects of a
governmental decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the
business entity." There is therefore a presumption that the decision regarding the north shore
area will have a material financial effect on RDC.

All others of your economic interests are indirectly involved. We do not have
information regarding the size of the business entities, and have assumed that they fit within the
regulation below.

Regulation 18705.1 (c) (enclosed) provides the materiality standard for indirectly
involved business entities. For a relatively small business, the standard is provided under (c)(4):

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

6 Our analysis of this issue is limited to the fact provided. With further facts supporting the diversity of
interests of the property owners in this area, the result could change.

7 We base this analysis on your estimate of the lease amount and the amount mentioned in supplemental
letters, as well as the assumption that after performing the calculation described above, you have received (or been
entitled to receive) at least $500 from Genser & Watkins through your interest in Baltic Development Associates
and its interest in the joint ventures.

File No. A-11-115
Page No. 8

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
(Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

Based on this information, you can determine whether there is an effect on your
indirectly involved economic interests.

Your communications with us have consistently stated that the property value of those
properties that could be re-zoned through this process will be affected. It is reasonably
foreseeable, based on your own assertions, that the decision will at the least have a material
financial effect on the RDC.

Steps Seven and Eight: "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation"
Exceptions

The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision despite a
conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707.) In other words, the
exception would apply if the decision will affect a significant segment of the public in
substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official's interests.

Section 87101 allows an official, who is otherwise disqualified to participate in a
governmental decision, if the official's participation is "legally required." (Section 87101;
Regulation 18708.) This exception is narrowly construed and applies only when it is legally
impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and
where there is no "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision." (Regulation 18708(a), (c) and (d); In re Tobias (1999) 13
FPPC Ops. 5.) Typically this exception is invoked when, due to disqualification, an agency is
unable to convene a "quorum" of its members.

It does not appear either of these exceptions applies to your facts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act permit you to participate in a decision regarding the re-zoning of the north 
shore area in Richmond?
sections.conclusion:
No. It appears from your facts that you have an economic interest in a business entity 
that is the subject of the decision.

Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 We base the advice below on the information you provided in your request for advice - we have taken 
into account the information provided by SSL Law Firm, representing Richmond Development Corp. Given the 
complexity of the business relationships, we have made every attempt to analyze the facts based on the several 
letters we received.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;  
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in any given governmental decision. 
Step One: Are you a "public official?" 
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections 
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee 
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .. " (Section 82048.) As a member of the 
City Council, you are a public official within the meaning of the Act. 3 
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision? 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, 
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive 
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision-maker 
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the 
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) You would be making, participating in making, or influencing a 
governmental decision when taking part in a City Council decision regarding Richmond's north 
shore. 
Step Three: What are your economic interests? 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows: 
• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she 
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); 
Regulation 18703.l(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); 
Regulation 18703 .1 (b) ); 
3 If a publiP official's office is listed in Section 87200, which specifically includes a city councilmember, 
and the official has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, the official must: ( 1) verbally 
identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as 
discussed in Regulation 18702.S(b )(I )(B), on the record of the meeting and immediately prior to the discussion of 
the item; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the 
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences, and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal 
interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5 subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87 !05.)  
• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a 
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b ); Regulation 
18703.2); 
• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including 
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the 
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3); 
• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if 
the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision 
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4); 
• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, 
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial 
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5). 
You have explained several business relationships that may be an economic interest to 
you. We discuss each in tum. 
Interactive Resources. Inc.: you have a 32.58 percent interest in this architectural firm. 
You are also its president. You have an economic interest in IRI as a business entity and as a 
source of income. 
Baltic Development Associates: you have a 10.23 percent interest in this limited liability 
partnership. You have stated that this interest is worth more than $2,000. There are several 
limited partners involved and none has a controlling interest. You have an economic interest in 
BDA as a business entity. 
Baltic Square Joint Venture: You have an economic interest in BDA, which is a 50 
percent owner of BSJV. RDC is the other 50 percent owner. You have stated that your 10.23 
percent interest in BDA would give you an investment worth more than $2,000 in BSJV. You 
have an economic interest in the BSJV as a business entity. 
Any lessee4 of the properties that BSJV owns would be an economic interest as a source 
of income to BDA. The amount of income would be calculated to account for BDA's 50 percent 
ownership of the BSJV and your 10.23'percent interest in BDA. For example, if a tenant pays 
$1,000 in rent, $500 of that is attributed to BDA and 10.23 percent of that $500 would be 
attributed as income, or $51.15.5 
4 See below for discussion of Genser & Watkins, LLP, as a lessee of BSJV. 
5 Please note that this is the calculation whether the joint ventures turn a net profit or not. We look only at 
the gross income when analyzing potential economic interests. We also realize that you have stated you have a 
5 . 1 15 percent interest in each joint venture. We do not see a reason to split your interest in half for this purpose. 
Rather, you would analyze a 10.23 percent interest in the income each receives.  
Mariner Square Joint Venture: MSJV is the same as BSJV above, except for the 
additional fact that MSJV paid IRI $332.66 for services rendered within the last 12 months. 
Your interest in Interactive Resources is 32.58 percent and that amount would be adjusted 
accordingly to $108.38. To be an economic interest as a source of income, the amount must total 
at least $500 in the past 12 months. This amount does not meet the threshold under the Act. 
You have not referred to any other payments specific to MSJV. 
Genser & Watkins. LLP: Genser & Watkins LLP is a legal partnership composed of two 
joint (50 percent) partners, including Joshua Genser. The law firm leases office space from 
BSJV. Genser & Watkins, LLP is a source of income to BDA using the same calculation as 
above: 50 percent of that income is attributed to BDA, and of that, 10.23 percent is attributed to 
you. The firm pays about $2,600 per month in rent. Your pro rata share of BDA's 50% share 
would be about $1,595 per year.

business relationship between one of your economic interests and this company. There is a

Mr. Genser has a 20 percent interest in the RDC. He runs that business out of his law 
office, using his firm's legal staff and resources. He is also the manager and day-to-day 
. decision-maker for RDC. 
Regulation 18703. l(d)(2) provides: 
"Business entities, including corporations, partnerships, joint ventures and any 
other organizations and enterprises operated for profit, which do not have a 
parent-subsidiary relationship are otherwise related if any one of the following 
three tests is met: 
"(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership interest in the other 
business entity. 
"(B) There is shared management and control between the entities. In 
determining whether there is shared management and control, consideration 
should be given to the following factors: 
"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages the 
two entities; 
"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets; 
"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees, or 
otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on a regular basis;  
"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between the 
entities; or 
"(C) A controlling owner (50 percent or greater interest as a shareholder or as a 
general partner) in one entity also is a controlling owner in the other entity." 
RDC is managed by Josh Genser who is a partner in Genser & Watkins, LLP. 
The businesses use the same principal place of business, the same staff, and Mr. Genser is 
a manager of RDC and partner in Genser & Watkins. These two entities are "otherwise 
related." You therefore have an interest in RDC as it is related to Genser & Watkins, a 
source of income to BDA. 
To summarize, you have an economic interest in IRI, BDA, Genser & Watkins, 
LLP, BSJV, and MSJV. You do not have a direct economic interest in RDC. RDC is 
otherwise related to Genser & Watkins, LLP, however, and RDC therefore "stands in the 
shoes" of the firm and any interest in the firm is imputed to RDC. 
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the 
governmental decision? 
A business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency 
when that person, either directly or by an agent: 
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by 
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding 
conc;eming the decision before the official or the official's agency. A 
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 
18704.l(a).) 
None of the listed economic interests above have initiated the proceeding or are 
named parties in the decision. None are "the subject" of the proceeding, except RDC to 
the extent that the decision involves "other entitlement to" the property. Because the 
decision involves a possible re-zoning of one of RDC' s holdings, it could be "the subject 
of the decision." 
While "other entitlement" is not specifically defined, the California Court of 
Appeal in City of Agoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation Com., (1988) 198 Cal. App. 
3d 480, 497-498 explained in the context of campaign contribution/disqualification that 
"entitlement for use" does not cover proceedings in which general policy decisions or 
mles are made or where the interests affected are many and diverse. (Citing Fallon  
Advice Letter, No. A-85-050.) Analogously, the "other entitlement" language above 
would apply to situations where "the interests are many and diverse."6 
Richmond's north shore area consists of approximately 100 acres and is split into 
properties held by 13 entities. Four of these property owners have expressed that they 
oppose the re-zoning of Richmond's north shore. Given these small numbers and lack of 
evidence of "diversity of interests," we find that your economic interest in RDC as a 
source of income is directly involved in the decision on the north shore area.7 
The remaining economic interests, above, are indirectly involved in the decision. 
Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably 
foreseeable that the standard will be met? 
For a business entity that is directly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation 
18705.1 (b)(l) states unless the exception in subdivision (b)(2) applies, "the financial effects of a 
governmental decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the governmental 
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not 
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the 
business entity." There is therefore a presumption that the decision regarding the north shore 
area will have a material financial effect on RDC. 
All others of your economic interests are indirectly involved. We do not have 
information regarding the size of the business entities, and have assumed that they fit within the 
regulation below. 
Regulation 18705.1 (c) (enclosed) provides the materiality standard for indirectly 
involved business entities. For a relatively small business, the standard is provided under (c)(4): 
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in 
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or 
more; or, 
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity 
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing 
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or, 
6 Our analysis of this issue is limited to the fact provided. With further facts supporting the diversity of 
interests of the property owners in this area, the result could change. 
7 We base this analysis on your estimate of the lease amount and the amount mentioned in supplemental 
letters, as well as the assumption that after performing the calculation described above, you have received (or been 
entitled to receive) at least $500 from Genser & Watkins through your interest in Baltic Development Associates 
and its interest in the joint ventures.  
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in 
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." 
(Regulation 18705.l(c)(4).) 
Based on this information, you can determine whether there is an effect on your 
indirectly involved economic interests. 
Your communications with us have consistently stated that the property value of those 
properties that could be re-zoned through this process will be affected. It is reasonably 
foreseeable, based on your own assertions, that the decision will at the least have a material 
financial effect on the RDC. 
Steps Seven and Eight: "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation" 
Exceptions 
The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision despite a 
conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's interest is not distinguishable 
from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707 .) In other words, the 
exception would apply if the decision will affect a significant segment of the public in 
substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official's interests. 
Section 87101 allows an official, who is otherwise disqualified to participate in a 
governmental decision, if the official's participation is "legally required." (Section 87101; 
Regulation 18708.) This exception is narrowly construed and applies only when it is legally 
impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and 
where there is no "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the 
statute authorizing the decision." (Regulation 18708(a), (c) and (d); In re Tobias (1999) 13 
FPPC Ops. 5.) Typically this exception is invoked when, due to disqualification, an agency is 
unable to convene a "quorum" of its members. 
It does not appear either of these exceptions applies to your facts.

QUESTION: Does the Act permit you to participate in a decision regarding the re-zoning of the north 
shore area in Richmond?

CONCLUSION: No. It appears from your facts that you have an economic interest in a business entity 
that is the subject of the decision. 
The regulations of the· Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 We base the advice below on the information you provided in your request for advice - we have taken 
into account the information provided by SSL Law Firm, representing Richmond Development Corp. Given the 
complexity of the business relationships, we have made every attempt to analyze the facts based on the several 
letters we received.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 29, 2011 Tom Butt City Council Member 117 Park Place Richmond, CA 94801 Re: Your Request for Advice Our file No. A-11-115 Dear Mr. Butt: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. Also, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice.² (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 72.) QUESTION Does the Act permit you to participate in a decision regarding the re-zoning of the north shore area in Richmond? CONCLUSION No. It appears from your facts that you have an economic interest in a business entity that is the subject of the decision. _________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² We base the advice below on the information you provided in your request for advice – we have taken into account the information provided by SSL Law Firm, representing Richmond Development Corp. Given the complexity of the business relationships, we have made every attempt to analyze the facts based on the several letters we received. File No. A-11-115 Page No. 2 FACTS Interactive Resources ("IRI"): You are the Vice Mayor for the City of Richmond. You are also the president of IRI, an architectural firm organized as a California corporation. You have a 32.58 percent interest in that firm. You assume for purposes of this advice that your interest in IRI has a value of more than $2,000. Baltic Development Associates ("BDA"): You are a limited partner in BDA with a 10.23 percent interest. You assume for purposes of this advice that your interest in BDA has a value of more than $2,000. BDA was created as a limited partnership in 1985. It currently has several limited partners, including yourself, that collectively hold a 100 percent interest in the partnership. IRI is the general partner of BDA for purposes of organization. You have stated that IRI has no financial interest or decision-making authority in BDA. Mariner Square Joint Venture ("MSJV") and Baltic Square Joint Venture ("BSJV"): BDA entered into two joint ventures with Richmond Development Company ("RDC"): MSJV and BSJV. BDA and RDC each have a 50 percent interest in each joint venture. Richmond Development Company ("RDC") and Genser & Watkins, LLP: Josh Genser has a controlling interest in and manages RDC and is
